\title{\vspace{-1.5cm}\textbf{\Huge agent-runtime}\\[0.3cm]
\Large Brief: an event-sourced runtime for tool-using LLM agents}
\author{A concise, accurate overview}
\date{}

\begin{document}

% interword space over a line that overflows into the margin and gets clipped.
\sloppy
\emergencystretch=3em

\thispagestyle{empty}

\begin{keyidea}{Scope of this document}
A condensed but accurate account of \file{agent-runtime}: what it is, the one idea it
is built on, what that idea buys, and where it leaks. Claims that could be checked by
running the code were checked; the transcripts here are real output. Terms are defined
in the glossary at the end rather than inline.

For the full treatment, every module and every function of consequence, see the
companion \file{deep-dive.pdf} in this folder. This is not an excerpt of it: it is the
same project from higher up.
\end{keyidea}

\tableofcontents
\newpage

% ============================================================
\section{The problem it picks}
% ============================================================

Writing an agent loop is easy. A model is asked what to do, it names a tool, the
program runs the tool, the result goes back to the model, repeat until it answers.
That is a weekend.

What is hard is everything that makes such a thing safe to deploy:

\begin{itemize}
  \item What exactly happened in run 4187, and can I reproduce it?
  \item Did the agent email a customer before anyone approved it?
  \item If I upgrade the model, do the 24 flows that worked last week still work?
  \item It crashed halfway. Can I resume without sending that email twice?
\end{itemize}

None of those are questions about the loop. They are questions about the record. This
project's bet is that if you get the record right, all four stop being separate
features.

\begin{keyidea}{The single design decision}
Every run is an \textbf{append-only log of events} in SQLite. The full state of a run
(status, conversation, pending approvals, executed tools, cost) is a pure function of
that log, computed on demand. Nothing else is authoritative: no session object
survives a process, and none needs to.

Everything else in this document is a consequence of that sentence.
\end{keyidea}

Storing the log instead of the current state costs disk and a little speed. In return
it answers questions that overwriting cannot answer at all: what was true at step 6,
why did it do that, do it again exactly. For a component that is nondeterministic,
expensive per step, and acts irreversibly on the world, that trade is heavily
favorable. An agent is exactly such a component.

% ============================================================
\section{Architecture}
% ============================================================

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\resizebox{0.95\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=1.1cm and 1.1cm]
  \node[box, minimum width=3.4cm] (cli) {CLI / Web UI / scenario runner};
  \node[box, fill=softgrey, draw=accent, very thick, below=1.4cm of cli]
    (rt) {\textbf{Runtime}: the agent loop};
  % Lay the three collaborators out from the centre one, so they cannot overlap.
  \node[box, below=1.6cm of rt] (exec) {ToolExecutor\\+ ToolRegistry};
  \node[box, left=0.9cm of exec] (adapter)
    {ModelAdapter\\mock / anthropic\\/ openai};
  \node[box, right=0.9cm of exec] (appr) {Approval policy};
  \node[store, below=3.2cm of rt, minimum width=5.4cm] (st)
    {\textbf{EventStore} (SQLite)\\append-only log + idempotency ledger};
  \node[box, right=2.2cm of st, fill=white, draw=okgreen] (proj)
    {Projection\\log $\rightarrow$ RunState};

  \draw[flow] (cli) -- (rt);
  \draw[flow] (rt) -- (adapter);
  \draw[flow] (rt) -- (exec);
  \draw[flow] (rt) -- (appr);
  \draw[flow] (adapter) -- (st);
  \draw[flow] (exec) -- (st);
  \draw[flow] (appr) -- (st);
  % The runtime appends directly too. Route this around the left, so it does not
  % strike through the ToolExecutor box on its way down.
  \draw[flow] (rt.west) to[out=180, in=90] ($(adapter.north west)+(-0.8,0.25)$)
    to[out=270, in=180] (st.west);
  \draw[dflow] (st) -- (proj);
  \draw[dflow] (proj.north) to[out=90,in=0]
    node[lbl, pos=0.6]{every decision\\re-derived} (rt.east);
\end{tikzpicture}
}
\caption{Solid arrows append; dashed arrows read through the projection. No SQL
statement in \file{store.py} updates or deletes an event row.}
\end{figure}

\file{projection.py} is the single module that turns a log into meaning. The CLI, the
web UI, the replay comparator and the scenario assertions all consume its
\code{RunState}, so they cannot disagree about a run's status. This is enforced by
structure rather than convention: \file{events.py}, \file{store.py} and
\file{projection.py} sit at the bottom of the dependency graph and import nothing from
the rest of the package, so no other module is even positioned to reinterpret a log.

The loop itself, \code{Runtime.drive()}, repeats four steps: project the log; if
terminal, stop; resolve pending tool calls in order (read-only ones execute,
side-effecting ones need an approval decision or the run pauses); otherwise ask the
model for the next turn and append what happened.

\begin{keyidea}{Why this collapses three features into one}
Because step one re-derives everything from the log, ``resume after a crash'',
``continue after a human approves'' and ``take the next ordinary step'' are not three
code paths. They are one. There is no recovery logic anywhere in the codebase, because
there is nothing to recover: the loop cannot tell whether the last event was written
one millisecond or one week ago.

That is the practical payoff of the whole design, and the reason the recovery tests
exercise the normal path instead of a bespoke one.
\end{keyidea}

% ============================================================
\section{The event log}
% ============================================================

One table, \code{events(run\_id, seq, type, ts, payload)}, primary key
\code{(run\_id, seq)}. Twelve event types cover a run's whole life: \code{run\_created},
\code{model\_requested}, \code{model\_responded}, \code{model\_error},
\code{tool\_requested}, \code{tool\_approved}, \code{tool\_denied},
\code{tool\_executed}, \code{tool\_failed}, \code{checkpoint},
\code{run\_finished} and \code{run\_failed}.

Appends compute \code{MAX(seq)+1} and retry on the uniqueness violation, so two
writers racing on one run cannot both claim a sequence number, with no lock involved.
Every event has a \emph{canonical} JSON encoding (sorted keys, no whitespace) so two
logs can be compared for exact equality. That is what lets replay say
``byte-identical'' rather than ``looks about right''.

A real 15-event run, produced by actually running the shipped demo scenario:

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\resizebox{0.9\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=0.3cm and 0,
  ev/.style={rectangle, draw=inkblue, fill=softgrey, text=inkblue, align=left,
             font=\scriptsize\ttfamily, minimum width=10.6cm, minimum height=0.46cm,
             inner sep=3pt, rounded corners=2pt},
  gate/.style={ev, fill=white, draw=warnred, text=warnred},
  fin/.style={ev, fill=white, draw=okgreen, text=okgreen}]
  \node[ev] (e0) {seq 0 \ \ run\_created \ \ \ \ agent=ops\_assistant};
  \node[ev, below=of e0] (e1) {seq 1 \ \ model\_requested \ call\_number=1};
  \node[ev, below=of e1] (e2) {seq 2 \ \ model\_responded \ tool\_calls=[read\_file]};
  \node[ev, below=of e2] (e3) {seq 3 \ \ tool\_requested \ side\_effect=false};
  \node[ev, below=of e3] (e4) {seq 4 \ \ tool\_executed \ \ result=\{invoice rows\}};
  \node[ev, below=of e4] (e5) {seq 5 \ \ checkpoint};
  \node[ev, below=of e5] (e6) {seq 6 \ \ model\_requested \ call\_number=2};
  \node[ev, below=of e6] (e7) {seq 7 \ \ model\_responded \ tool\_calls=[draft\_email]};
  \node[gate, below=of e7] (e8) {seq 8 \ \ tool\_requested \ side\_effect=TRUE};
  \node[gate, below=of e8] (e9) {seq 9 \ \ tool\_approved \ \ approver=cli};
  \node[ev, below=of e9] (e10) {seq 10 \ tool\_executed \ \ draft\_path=outbox/....eml};
  \node[ev, below=of e10] (e11) {seq 11 \ checkpoint};
  \node[ev, below=of e11] (e12) {seq 12 \ model\_requested \ call\_number=3};
  \node[ev, below=of e12] (e13) {seq 13 \ model\_responded \ final text, no tool calls};
  \node[fin, below=of e13] (e14) {seq 14 \ run\_finished \ \ \ final\_answer="Drafted a reminder..."};
  \draw[decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=5pt}, draw=warnred, thick]
    ($(e9.north east)+(0.2,0.05)$) -- ($(e8.south east)+(0.2,-0.05)$)
    node[midway, right=6pt, align=left, font=\scriptsize\itshape, text=warnred]
    {the run stopped here\\until a human approved};
\end{tikzpicture}
}
\caption{The gate is the only place a run waits, and it waits by stopping, not by
blocking.}
\end{figure}

% ============================================================
\section{What the log buys}
% ============================================================

\subsection{The approval gate}

Side-effecting tools (write a file, update a CSV, draft an email, add a calendar entry)
stop the run. An \emph{approval policy} is just a function from a call to
\code{approve}, \code{deny(reason)}, or \code{None} meaning ``ask a human''.

The important part is what a pending approval \emph{is}: not a blocked thread and not
an in-memory queue, but a fact about the log, namely a \code{tool\_requested} event
with \code{side\_effect: true} and no decision event after it. The process can exit.
Any process, days later, can read the log and see it. That is why the CLI and the web
UI serve the same queue while sharing nothing but a file. A denial is fed back to the
model as an ordinary observation (\code{\{"denied": true, "reason": ...\}}), so ``the
operator said no'' is something the agent can react to rather than an error.

\subsection{Crash safety}

The executor derives an idempotency key from \code{(run\_id, seq of the tool\_requested
event)}. Both are identical on resume, so the key is stable across the crash it is meant
to survive. It writes the result to an \code{INSERT OR IGNORE} ledger \emph{before} the
runtime appends \code{tool\_executed}; a resumed run finds the ledger entry and reports
\code{replayed: true} instead of re-running the handler.
\file{tests/test\_resume.py} crashes a run in that window and asserts a side-effect
counter stays at 1.

\subsection{Deterministic replay}

\code{agentrun replay <run-id>} rebuilds a run from four recorded stand-ins: a clock
replaying the original timestamps in append order, an adapter replaying recorded turns
and errors, an executor replaying recorded tool outcomes, and an approval policy
replaying recorded decisions. The \emph{real, unmodified} runtime then drives the loop.
Nothing is swapped except the run's inputs, which is the point: a loop reimplemented
for replay would prove nothing about the loop.

\begin{keyidea}{Why replay matters more for an agent than for ordinary software}
Suppose a run drafted an email to the wrong customer and you want to know why. You
cannot just run it again: the model is nondeterministic, so a second run may behave
perfectly and tell you nothing; it costs money every attempt; and it would draft another
email, so your debugging tool has side effects.

Replay dissolves all three. The log already holds every nondeterministic input the run
received. Feed those back and the run becomes a pure function: same events every time,
free, no email sent, steppable, a year later.

This works only because every event payload was designed so replay can recompute it. The
store takes an injectable clock, sleeps become no-ops, and \code{model\_requested}
stores a digest rather than anything time-dependent. Determinism was spent field by
field, which is what lets the comparator demand exact equality instead of ``close
enough''.
\end{keyidea}

Verified, not quoted:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ agentrun replay 46bd97a45451
replayed 15/15 events into 46bd97a45451-replay-8765c6
byte-identical: yes
\end{lstlisting}

\subsection{Failure injection}

A YAML-declarable \code{FaultPlan} names faults at points: model faults per adapter call
(\code{malformed}, \code{adapter\_500}) and tool faults per attempt (\code{timeout},
\code{exception}). The contract: adapter errors retry with backoff then fail the run with
a recorded cause; malformed output earns two corrective observations then fails; tool
failures surface to the model as ordinary error observations. Nothing escapes
\code{drive()} as an unhandled exception.

\subsection{Regression testing between model versions}

\begin{keyidea}{What is actually being tested}
You cannot unit-test ``the agent does the right thing'', because the agent's behavior
lives in a model you do not control. So the project tests the loop against a
\emph{recorded} behavior: a scenario names behavior versions, each a full script of what
the model would say, and asserts what the runtime does with it.

That turns an unanswerable question into an answerable one: not ``is the agent correct''
but ``if the model behaves like this, does the runtime do the right thing, and does
\code{v2} differ from \code{v1}, and where exactly''. Offline, in seconds, for free.
\end{keyidea}

24 YAML scenarios ship, 8 per agent. Each has a request, optional fixture files, named
behaviors, optional faults and approvals policy, and an \code{expect} block: exact or
partial tool lists, denied tools, final-answer substrings, failure cause, files created,
minimum event count, and a structural check that no side effect executed before its
approval. Verified:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ agentrun test scenarios/ --behavior v1
behavior v1: 24/24 scenarios passed

$ agentrun test scenarios/ --behavior v1 --compare v2
comparing v1 -> v2: v2 fails 3 of 24 scenarios (3 regressions vs v1)
  [REGRESSION] chase-overdue-acme
      first divergence at event 3: expected tool_requested:read_file,
                                        got tool_requested:draft_email
\end{lstlisting}

The \code{v2} behaviors are deliberately degraded: this one drafts the email without
reading the invoice first. The mechanism is pointedly simple. Each event is reduced to a
\code{"type:detail"} string and two lists are walked in lock-step to the first mismatch.
No diffing library, because for an ordered list of short strings, first-mismatch is the
complete answer. That single line is the most useful output the tool produces.

% ============================================================
\section{Tools, adapters, agents}
% ============================================================

Each tool declares a JSON-schema for its arguments, a \code{side\_effect} flag, a
timeout and a retry budget. The executor validates arguments, runs the handler under a
timeout, and splits failures two ways: \code{ToolError} is an expected deterministic
refusal (sandbox escape, allowlist miss, missing file) and fails fast, since retrying
cannot help; anything else is transient and consumes the retry budget with backoff. That
one distinction buys the whole retry policy. All file paths resolve through a workspace
sandbox that rejects escapes.

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{2.5cm}p{7.6cm}p{1.5cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Tool} & \textbf{What it does} & \textbf{Side effect} \\
\midrule
\code{read\_file} & read a UTF-8 text file & no \\
\code{write\_file} & write a UTF-8 text file & yes \\
\code{list\_files} & recursively list files & no \\
\code{http\_get} & GET an allowlisted URL & no \\
\code{csv\_update} & append a row or set a cell in a CSV & yes \\
\code{draft\_email} & write an \code{.eml} draft file; never sends & yes \\
\code{calendar\_add} & insert into a per-workspace SQLite calendar & yes \\
\code{calendar\_list} & read the calendar back & no \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

One interface, \code{ModelAdapter.complete(messages, tools) $\rightarrow$ ModelTurn},
has three implementations: \code{MockModelAdapter} (a deterministic offline script
player), \code{AnthropicAdapter} (Messages API) and \code{OpenAIAdapter} (Chat
Completions), both env-key-driven over \code{httpx} and both accepting an injectable
client, which is what makes them testable with no network.

Three example agents each pair a system prompt with a restricted tool subset, enforced by
the registry rather than by the prompt: \code{ops\_assistant} (chases overdue invoices,
drafts reminders, never sends), \code{data\_entry} (transcribes to CSV, verifies by
reading back), \code{research\_summarizer} (fetches allowlisted pages and notes, writes a
sourced summary).

The web UI deserves one line beyond its existence: pending approvals are rendered as a
real unified diff of what the file would become, not as a blob of JSON arguments. An
approval gate showing JSON gets rubber-stamped within a day. One showing the actual diff
is a control someone might really read.

% ============================================================
\section{Verification: what was actually run}
% ============================================================

The README claims ``93 tests, all offline'' and that no live API calls were made. Both
were checked rather than trusted.

\begin{lstlisting}
$ pytest -q
93 passed, 1 warning in 46.80s
\end{lstlisting}

13 test files, 1357 lines, 93 functions matching \verb|^def test_|, and
\code{pytest --collect-only} reports 93 collected. There is no \code{parametrize}
anywhere in \file{tests/}, so the count is not inflated: the README's number is exact.
The single warning comes from FastAPI's \code{TestClient}, inside the library, not from
this project.

A passing offline suite does not by itself prove nothing called out: a call could succeed
silently or be swallowed by a retry. So the suite was re-run with a guard patching
\code{socket.socket.connect}, \code{socket.create\_connection} and
\code{socket.getaddrinfo} to raise on any non-loopback address:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ pytest -q -p noweb
NETWORK GUARD: zero outbound connections or DNS lookups attempted.
93 passed, 1 warning in 55.01s
\end{lstlisting}

Not one outbound connection, and not even a DNS lookup. The claim is stronger than the
README states it: the suite does not merely avoid calling the providers, it never
resolves their hostnames.

Beyond the suite, the CLI was driven end to end: a run pausing at the gate, a CLI
approval resuming it to \code{finished}, a full replay reporting
\code{byte-identical: yes}, a \code{--until} replay reporting mid-run state, 24/24
scenarios passing on \code{v1}, and the \code{v1} against \code{v2} diff reporting 3
regressions with first-divergence event numbers. Every transcript in this document is
real output.

\begin{keyidea}{Why the offline property is architecture, not luck}
Nothing is skipped for lack of a key and nothing is patched at the last moment. The
adapters take an injectable client, the runtime an injectable adapter, the store an
injectable clock, the executor an injectable sleep and fault hook. Offline falls out of
the constructor signatures.

That is also its honest limit: the same design that runs 93 tests with no network is why
none of them can tell you the live adapters work.
\end{keyidea}

% ============================================================
\section{Honest findings}
% ============================================================

Running and reading the code surfaced defects the README does not mention. Each is
reproduced and evidenced in the deep dive at the section given.

\begin{honest}{A truncated replay can execute a real side effect (Deep Dive Sec. 9.4)}
Replay writes a full duplicate run into the \emph{same} database, copying the original's
metadata verbatim, marked as a replay only by a substring in its run id that no other
module checks. Replay with \code{--until} truncated at a pending side-effecting call and
the replayed run stops at \code{waiting\_approval}, leaving a phantom entry in the real
approval queue:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ agentrun approvals list
d5ca78675af3-replay-6c2258  call-2-0  draft_email  {'to': 'billing@acme.example', ...}
\end{lstlisting}

Approving it rebuilds a runtime from that copied metadata, with a \emph{real} executor
over the \emph{real} workspace, and the tool fires for real. Confirmed by deleting the
outbox first, so a reappearing file could only mean the handler genuinely ran; it
reappeared. The idempotency ledger cannot help, since its key includes the run id and the
replay's is different.

``Replay: no network, no tool handlers'' is true \emph{during} replay. It is not true
afterwards, because a truncated replay leaves behind a live, resumable run that no longer
knows it was ever a replay. Either mark replayed runs in their payload and have resume
and the queue refuse them, or replay into a throwaway database. The second is cleaner: a
replay is a question, not a fact, and has no business being stored beside real runs.
\end{honest}

\begin{honest}{Resume rewinds the script after a scripted malformed turn (Deep Dive Sec. 18)}
The mock adapter advances its script index before inspecting the turn, so a \emph{scripted}
malformed turn consumes a script slot. But \code{resume\_run} repositions that index by
counting \code{model\_responded} events that are \textbf{not} malformed, so it does not
count that turn. Resume therefore rewinds by one and serves the malformed turn again,
which can fail a run that would otherwise have completed.

The README concedes a nearby case (injected faults realigning off by one) but not this
one. Two shipped scenarios use scripted malformed turns; neither is ever resumed by the
tests, which is why 93 tests pass over a real bug.

The generalization is the interesting part. The project's thesis is ``all state is a
function of the log''. The script cursor is the one piece of state that is not, and it is
inferred by a heuristic rather than recorded. Every feature that reads the log is exact;
the one component that guesses is the one with a latent bug. That is an argument
\emph{for} the discipline, not against it. The README already names the fix: record the
cursor in the \code{checkpoint} payload, which is currently carrying only numbers that
were recomputable anyway.
\end{honest}

\begin{honest}{The crash window is narrower than claimed, not closed (Deep Dive Sec. 8)}
The ledger write happens on the line after the handler returns. A crash \emph{between}
those two statements leaves the side effect done and nothing recorded, and a resumed run
runs the handler again. The test does not cover it: \code{CrashAfterExecute} raises after
calling the inner executor \emph{in full}, ledger write included, so it crashes in the
window that is closed. Its docstring calls it ``the worst possible crash point'', which is
the one thing it is not.

This is defensible and worth defending precisely. The gap is inherent: you cannot
atomically perform a real-world side effect and a local database write. Shrinking the
window is the best a local ledger can do; closing it requires the side effect to carry the
idempotency key so the \emph{remote} system deduplicates, as payment APIs do. The honest
claim is ``the duplicate window is reduced to two adjacent statements'', not ``no duplicate
side effects''.
\end{honest}

Also found, and detailed in the deep dive: the web approval gate has no authentication or
CSRF protection (mitigated by the localhost default bind, but the gate \emph{is} the
security control this project is built around); the approval preview re-implements tool
logic rather than sharing it and already disagrees with the real tool on out-of-range
cells; \file{agentrun.db} is committed to the repository and the default database path
writes into the working tree; \code{except RuntimeError: pass} in replay swallows genuine
store and adapter errors and mislabels them as divergence; a too-short mock script is
reported as malformed \emph{model} output rather than as the scenario bug it is;
\code{draft\_email} silently overwrites drafts sharing a subject; and a hung tool does not
merely leak a thread, it hangs \code{execute()} itself past the timeout.

The README states these on its own: live adapters were never exercised against the real
APIs (no keys were available); web approvals resume synchronously in the request handler;
tool timeouts abandon rather than kill the worker thread; event payloads carry no schema
version; the price table is hardcoded and will drift.

\begin{keyidea}{The fair summary}
Every finding above is a defect in the operational shell. Not one is a defect in the
central idea. The event log, the projection as sole interpreter, the gate as an ordering
property rather than a lock, and byte-identical replay all hold up under examination and
under execution: 93 tests pass with provably zero network, replay is byte-identical on
real runs, and the regression diff does what it claims.

What the findings show is narrower and more interesting: the seams. The phantom approval
happens because a replay is written into the same table as a real run and distinguished
only by a naming convention. The resume bug happens because one piece of state was
inferred rather than recorded. Both are the project not taking its own discipline quite
far enough, which is a far better thing to defend than a design that does not work.

Of the limitations the README raises itself, the unversioned event payload is the one
most worth fixing first. For a project whose whole premise is that the log is the durable
asset, a log that cannot say which code wrote it is the one structural gap that is cheap
now and expensive later.
\end{keyidea}

% ============================================================
\section{Glossary}
% ============================================================
\begin{description}[leftmargin=3.2cm, style=nextline]
\item[Adapter] A class implementing a fixed interface around a specific model API or a fake.
\item[Approval gate] The point where a side-effecting call pauses for a human decision.
\item[Canonical encoding] A serialization where equal data always produces an identical string.
\item[Contract test] A test verifying request and response shape against an API's spec without calling it.
\item[Deterministic] Same inputs, same outputs, every time.
\item[Event sourcing] Storing every state change as an immutable ordered log, and computing current state from it on demand, instead of overwriting stored state.
\item[Fault injection] Deliberately simulating failures to test that they are handled.
\item[Idempotent] Doing something twice has the same effect as doing it once.
\item[JSON Schema] A specification language describing the required shape of JSON data.
\item[LLM] Large language model: a model that generates text or structured output from a prompt.
\item[Projection] Computing derived state from an event log.
\item[Replay] Re-driving logic using only recorded data, with no live calls, checking the result matches.
\item[Sandbox] A restricted environment limiting what a program can access.
\item[Side effect] An action with a lasting, observable consequence outside the program.
\item[SQLite] A serverless relational database engine whose whole database is one file.
\item[Unified diff] A compact standard format for showing line-level differences between two texts.
\end{description}

\end{document}
